one_line_takeaway: CausalRM trains RLHF reward models from cheap observational user feedback by jointly correcting annotation noise (noise-aware surrogate loss) and selection bias (propensity reweighting), yielding provably unbiased estimators and measurable downstream safety-alignment gains (+49.2% WildGuardMix, +32.7% HarmBench, Causal-DR/Qwen2.5-7B).
key_finding: CausalRM (especially the DR variant) achieves state-of-the-art observational reward modeling, and only jointly addressing both noise and bias works: debias-only methods (IPS/SDR) and denoise-only methods (F-correction/ILDE) each improve over Naive but remain suboptimal because they leave the other imperfection uncorrected. On semi-synthetic data with known r*, CausalRM-IPS/DR reach near-zero estimation bias (delta ~0.002-0.005) versus large bias for Naive (delta_MSE up to 0.282), empirically validating the unbiasedness theorems. Downstream, RMs trained with CausalRM produce safer aligned policies: the two headline numbers are both CausalRM-DR on Qwen2.5-7B relative to the Naive RM baseline (Table 5) -- +49.2% safety score on WildGuardMix (0.569 vs 0.381) and +32.7% on HarmBench (0.758 vs 0.571).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'observational reward modeling' as a formal problem: training RLHF reward models (RMs) from passively-collected observational user feedback (clicks, copies, upvotes, regenerates) rather than costly expert-annotated experimental preference data. Its own contribution is CausalRM, a causal-theoretic RM training framework that jointly corrects for two imperfections the authors formalize via the potential-outcome framework: (1) annotation noise (observed feedback r differs from latent true preference r*), handled by a noise-aware surrogate loss whose expectation under the noise distribution equals the primal loss (closed-form solution given false-positive rate rho10 and false-negative rate rho01, which are estimated via a weak-separability/anchor-unit assumption rather than assumed known); and (2) user preference bias (feedback is selectively provided, creating a train/inference distribution shift O != D), handled by propensity-score reweighting. The two corrections are combined into two estimators, CausalRM-IPS (inverse-propensity) and CausalRM-DR (doubly robust), which the authors prove are unbiased estimators of the ideal objective (Theorems 1-2) with DR having lower variance (Theorem 3). NOTE ON PRIOR WORK: standalone IPS (Rosenbaum & Rubin 1983), DR (Robins 1994), the Bradley-Terry model, and the weak-separability assumption (Menon 2015) are borrowed scaffolding / baselines, NOT contributions; the novelty is coupling the noise-aware surrogate loss WITH propensity reweighting for reward modeling.
threat_model: The paper does NOT develop an adversarial threat model, and none should be imputed. Its security relevance is indirect: safety alignment is the explicit downstream RLHF testbed (Section 4.7), evaluated on jailbreak/harm benchmarks (HarmBench, WildGuardMix, DAN). The paper mentions 'malicious system manipulation' once (as one possible source of annotation noise among inattention and impulsive clicks) but does not model an attacker, poisoning, or manipulation adversary. The work is best characterized as an RLHF/causal-inference methods paper whose primary application and evaluation is LLM safety alignment.
limitations: Two limitations stated by the authors. (1) Scope is limited to the RM learning OBJECTIVE; they do not explore architectures (e.g., Mixture-of-Experts) that jointly model preference and propensity. (2) They exclude the availability of experimental (expert-labeled) feedback; a proposed future direction is to combine the scalability of observational feedback with the quality of a modest amount of experimental feedback. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: the entire evaluation relies on SEMI-SYNTHETIC bias/noise injection (propensity and flip rates are synthetically generated, not measured from real deployment logs), so real-world observational feedback distributions may differ; noise-rate estimation depends on the weak-separability/anchor-unit assumption; and downstream safety scores are judged by a single LLM judge (DeepSeek-V3).

datasets_used: PKU-SafeRLHF (RM quality via Severity Level proxy; also RM-training source for downstream RLHF and basis for semi-synthetic experiments)
quantitative_results: Downstream (Table 5, Qwen2.5-7B policy, vs Naive): CausalRM-DR WildGuardMix 0.569 = +49.2%; HarmBench 0.758 = +32.7%; DAN 0.680 = +19.6%
Downstream (Table 5, Qwen2.5-7B): CausalRM-IPS HarmBench 0.741 (+29.7%), WildGuardMix 0.551 (+44.6%), DAN 0.663 (+16.7%)
RM quality Table 1 (PKU-SafeRLHF, Scenario 1 rho01=0.2 rho10=0.1): CausalRM-DR R2 0.779 / MSE 0.055 vs Naive R2 0.559 / MSE 0.110; best baseline SDR R2 0.705, ILDE R2 0.706
RM quality Table 1 (HelpSteer, Scenario 1): CausalRM-DR R2 0.283 vs Naive R2 0.090; (UltraFeedback) CausalRM-DR R2 0.495 vs Naive 0.405
Semi-synthetic unbiasedness (Table 3, PKU-SafeRLHF): CausalRM-IPS/DR delta ~0.002-0.005 across ROTATE/SKEW/ONES/FOURS vs Naive delta_MSE up to 0.282 on ROTATE
Ablation (Table 2, PKU-SafeRLHF): full CausalRM-DR R2 0.779 vs debias-only 0.644 vs denoise-only 0.663 vs Naive 0.559 -- both components needed
Abstract headline gains restated: 49.2% on WildGuardMix and 32.7% on HarmBench
baselines_compared: Debias-based: IPS, MTIPS, CVIB, DR, MTDR, SDR
Denoise-based: F-correction, Co-Teaching, CoDis, LabelWave, Robust DivideMix, SelectMix, ILDE
